cataloging_subject.label: The Tretis of the Tua Mariit Wemen and the Wedo
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The abstract 530-line Older Scots poem by William Dunbar. This descriptive record is not Kinsley's 1979 edition, the U-M encoding, either textual witness, or the repository XML representation.

catalog_summary.title: The Tretis of the Tua Mariit Wemen and the Wedo
catalog_summary.author: William Dunbar
catalog_summary.date: By the conjectural [1507?] first printing; exact composition date unknown
catalog_summary.region: Scotland (cultural origin; exact place of composition unknown)

titles.value: The Tretis of the Tua Mariit Wemen and the Wedo
The Tretis of the Twa Mariit Women and the Wedo
titles.supplied_by: University of Michigan CME / Kinsley source header
notes: The repository header uses this partially modernized spelling.

titles.value: Upon the Midsummer even merriest of nights
notes: Normalized for catalog display from the opening line; historical spelling varies by witness and edition.

agents.name: William Dunbar
notes: The manuscript rubric and explicit, and the early-print explicit, independently attribute the poem to Dunbar.

assertion.notes: Pepys MS 2553 gives an attribution at the rubric and an explicit naming 'maister Williame Dunbar'; the early print ends 'quod dunbar.'

date_statements.display: By the conjectural [1507?] first printing; exact date unknown

assertion.notes: The poem must predate its conjecturally dated [1507?] early-print attestation, but the print itself is undated and the cataloged year is uncertain.

notes: The XML profile's single year 1508 is inherited approximate metadata and is not treated as an exact composition date.

CELM's older statement that the work was printed at Edinburgh in 1508 by Chepman and Myllar conflicts with current DIMEV/EEBO cataloging and NLS's attribution guidance; it is retained as a competing attributed claim rather than merged into a date range.

assertion.notes: The sole surviving exemplar is imperfect and preserves only lines 104-530, but its existence attests the work.
Place, printer, and precise date are uncertain; the surviving print is not safely attributable to Chepman and Myllar.

places.label: Scotland
notes: This broad cultural-origin label does not assert an exact composition site or resolve the early print's disputed place of production.

assertion.notes: The work is by the Scottish poet William Dunbar and is transmitted in Older Scots; this does not establish a city or court as the exact place of composition.

language_statements.label: Older Scots
notes: Rickly explicitly describes the language of The Tretis as Middle Scots; this record uses the broader repository display label 'Older Scots' and ISO 639-3 code sco.
The CME XML itself contains no language declaration and is not used as evidence for this statement.

form_statements.description: A continuous poem in 530 unrhymed alliterative long lines, organized as speeches and verse paragraphs rather than a regular stanza sequence.

genre_statements.label: Debate poem
notes: The two married women and the widow exchange contrasting accounts of husbands, desire, and strategies within marriage.

genre_statements.label: Social satire
notes: METS frames the poem as social satire with fabliau-like elements; 'fabliau' is retained as a descriptive comparison, not promoted to a separate controlled genre here.

subject_statements.label: Courtly love and marriage
notes: DIMEV indexes a burlesque of courtly love; the poem's sustained subject is the women's experience and manipulation of marriage.

scope.description: The garden drinking gathering frames and enables the women's debate.

summaries.value: On a midsummer night, a concealed male narrator watches two married women and a widow drinking in a garden. Each woman recounts and debates her experience of husbands, sexuality, courtly behavior, and the tactics by which she negotiates marriage; the widow then develops the longest and most worldly account. The overheard female symposium forms an alliterative social satire on marriage and gendered speech.

This record describes one abstract poem. The six DIV1 ranges in the U-M XML are explicitly supplied electronic navigation groups, and its 26 LG elements are variable speech/verse-paragraph groupings, not 26 regular stanzas or six intellectual sections.

extent.description: One complete poem of 530 numbered verse lines. The XML contains 26 LG elements used for speeches and verse paragraphs, seven HEAD elements, and seventeen PB milestones spanning Kinsley pages 42-58. Six DIV1 containers and their line-range heads are explicitly supplied navigation units, not authorial chapters; the poem is continuous rather than regularly stanzaic.

content_parts.label: The Tretis of the Tua Mariit Wemen and the Wedo
content_parts.summary: The complete 530-line poem: an observer's garden frame, speeches by two married women, and the widow's extended speech.

locators.value: signatures a3-b3; poem lines 104-530; bound-volume images 177-189, with image 189 shared with the following work

notes: The first 103 lines do not survive in the sole early print but do survive in the Maitland Folio manuscript.
No subordinate content parts are created for the six supplied XML range divisions because they are not intellectual divisions of the poem.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_from_CTA/DunTwa.xml, header and supplied division heads.
evidence.locator: FILEDESC, SOURCEDESC, ENCODINGDESC, PROFILEDESC, and six DIV1/HEAD ranges
evidence.summary: The header names William Dunbar and Kinsley's 1979 source edition, gives an inherited creation date of 1508, and records a 1993 electronic publication. It labels the six top-level line-range divisions as supplied and records omission of front/back matter and editorial notes; it contains no language declaration.

evidence.citation: Computed structural inspection of CME/source/CME_from_CTA/DunTwa.xml at pinned CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: BODY element counts and line sequence
evidence.summary: The XML contains one poem, six explicitly supplied DIV1 ranges, seven HEAD elements, 26 LG elements, 530 L elements numbered 1-530, and seventeen PB milestones spanning printed pages 42-58. Reading the text confirms the garden drinking frame, two married women's speeches, and the widow's extended speech.

evidence.citation: DIMEV v1.0, record 6134, as documented in lineage:DunTwa.
evidence.summary: DIMEV supplies Dunbar attribution, the normalized incipit, 530 unrhymed alliterative long lines, the exact subjects 'women, against,' 'debates,' and 'courtly love, burlesque of,' and entries for the Maitland and imperfect early-print witnesses.

evidence.citation: Patricia Rickly, William Dunbar's 'Tretis of the Tua Mariit Wemen and the Wedo': A Critical Edition (1980), as documented in lineage:DunTwa.
evidence.summary: Rickly explicitly describes the language of The Tretis as Middle Scots and distinguishes the beginning-to-end but internally defective Maitland witness from the early print that lacks lines 1-103.

evidence.citation: CELM DuW 185, as documented in lineage:DunTwa.
evidence.summary: CELM independently confirms the poem's title/attribution and explicit naming William Dunbar in Pepys MS 2553, pages 81-96.

evidence.citation: Eve Salisbury, ed., The Trials and Joys of Marriage (2002), METS edition, as documented in lineage:DunTwa.
evidence.summary: The modern teaching edition and introduction support the poem's social-satire and debate framing, its treatment of marriage and courtly love, and the women's garden drinking community.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland First Scottish Books item list and facsimile, as documented in lineage:DunTwa.
evidence.summary: NLS identifies the early printed tract as an additional item not printed by Chepman and Myllar; its public images preserve poem lines 104-530 and the explicit 'quod dunbar.'

evidence.citation: EEBO-TCP A20974 header and transcription, as documented in lineage:DunTwa.
evidence.summary: The encoding identifies the imperfect STC 7350/ESTC S114713 tract, its conjectural '[Scotland? ... 1507?]' imprint, and the loss of all before signature a3.

open_questions.question: Can the poem's composition be dated more narrowly than 'by the conjectural [1507?] first printing' without treating the conjectural print date, inherited XML year 1508, or CELM's older Chepman-and-Myllar attribution as exact?
next_steps: Review current Dunbar chronology scholarship and the latest specialist study of the early print's typography and date.
Separate evidence for composition from evidence for first attestation in any revision.

open_questions.question: Is there reliable evidence for an exact place of composition within Scotland?
next_steps: Consult current Dunbar biographies and poem-specific scholarship for a grounded court, city, or itinerary claim.
Retain Scotland only as cultural origin unless evidence distinguishes composition place from author association and print production.

notes: The abstract work is the cataloging subject; James Kinsley is therefore not entered as editor of the work itself, although his edition is the immediate source in the linked lineage manifest.
The Maitland Folio and STC 7350 are two textual witnesses with different survival coverage. They are not merged into a single source entity.
The six supplied XML line ranges and 26 LG elements are encoding/verse-paragraph structures, not an imposed stanza count.
The early print's presence in the volume called the Chepman and Myllar Prints does not justify attribution to those printers.

The current preferred first-printing formulation is [1507?]. The XML year 1508 and CELM's older Edinburgh 1508/Chepman-and-Myllar statement remain attributed competing claims, not endpoints of a synthesized range.
